exploration_log.aha_moment: The hash uses `type` as the discriminator combined with the value hash. Since `value_t::number_integer != value_t::number_unsigned`, the combine inputs differ even for numerically equal values. Normalizing the non-negative integer case to use the unsigned type discriminator makes both paths produce the same output.
exploration_log.hindsight: Nothing to change; the fix was minimal and correct.

alternatives: Normalize both integer and unsigned to a shared type discriminator
Always cast to a common type (e.g., int64) for both
Only normalize non-negative integers to the unsigned path
key_decisions.decision: Normalize non-negative integers to the unsigned hash path
key_decisions.reasoning: operator== already considers json(1) and json(1u) equal; making the integer case reuse the unsigned type discriminator and cast is the minimal change; negative integers keep their distinct hash since they have no unsigned equivalent

search_sequence.reasoning: Task description explicitly names hash.hpp and the hash function
search_sequence.result: The function has separate cases for number_integer and number_unsigned, each using different type discriminators in combine() — the root of the inconsistency

solve_notes: In `hash.hpp`, the `number_integer` case used `value_t::number_integer` as the type discriminator combined with the signed hash, while `number_unsigned` used `value_t::number_unsigned`. For non-negative integers, I redirect to the unsigned path by casting the value to `number_unsigned_t` and using `value_t::number_unsigned` as the discriminator — matching exactly what the unsigned case produces. Negative integers are unchanged.
task_text:
Fix hash function producing different results for equivalent number types

The `hash()` function in `detail/hash.hpp` uses separate
`std::hash<number_integer_t>` and `std::hash<number_unsigned_t>`
specializations combined with different `value_t` discriminators for
integer and unsigned values. This means `json(1)` (signed) and
`json(1u)` (unsigned) produce different hashes despite comparing
equal with `operator==`. Fix `hash.hpp` to normalize equivalent
integer and unsigned values to the same hash by using a unified
hashing path when the values are numerically equal.

thrash_preventing_defs.name: combine
thrash_preventing_defs.reason: read: understanding that type is the first argument to combine explains why different value_t discriminators cause different hashes even for same numeric values

thrash_preventing_defs.name: value_t
thrash_preventing_defs.reason: read: confirms numeric values of value_t::number_integer and value_t::number_unsigned are distinct integers used as combine seeds

tier_difference_reasoning: The minimum set is just the `hash` function because that is all that was edited. The thrash_preventing set adds `combine` (to understand that the type discriminator feeds into the hash and that this is the root cause of the inconsistency) and `value_t` (to confirm the enum values are distinct, preventing an agent from assuming they share a value).
